content.text: When an agentic AI orchestrator calls a downstream tool service, a retrieval API, or an LLM proxy, it implicitly agrees to a contract: a set of expected request shapes, response schemas, and error codes that the provider must honor. PACT consumer-driven contract testing makes that implicit agreement explicit, version-controlled, and machine-verifiable — catching API breaking changes before they propagate to production and break multi-step agent workflows.
What it is

PACT is a consumer-driven contract testing framework. The consumer service (here, the agent orchestrator or LLM client) generates a pact file — a JSON document describing the interactions it expects from a provider: which HTTP endpoints it calls, what request body shape it sends, and what response shape it needs to function correctly. The provider service downloads the pact and runs a verification job that replays those interactions against its real implementation, confirming that it can satisfy the consumer's expectations.

In agentic AI architectures, the consumer-provider relationship appears in several places: the agent orchestrator calling a tool microservice that executes code, web searches, or database queries; the LLM client calling an internal API gateway that proxies to a foundation model provider; a RAG pipeline calling a vector store service; or an agent calling another agent via an API boundary. Each of these relationships benefits from a pact that locks the interface against unilateral provider changes.

PACT is consumer-driven rather than specification-driven: instead of maintaining a shared OpenAPI spec that both sides must manually keep in sync, the consumer generates the contract from its own test cases. The provider verifies against the consumer's actual expectations, not a hypothetical specification. This eliminates the common problem where a spec is correct but neither side implements it faithfully.
Why it matters

Microservice architectures for agentic AI systems are fragile at API boundaries. An agent orchestrator may call a tool service with a JSON body whose schema it learned during development. If the tool service team renames a field, changes a type, or adds a new required property, the agent will silently fail at runtime — producing a vague error deep inside a multi-turn reasoning loop where the cause is hard to trace.

Traditional integration testing partially addresses this, but only for combinations that test authors anticipated. Contract testing provides a structural guarantee: if the provider's verification passes, any interaction the consumer expressed in the pact will be satisfied by the provider's current implementation. The guarantee holds even for field-level changes that integration tests might miss if the test fixture happened to work with either the old or new schema.

For agentic AI systems, the stakes are elevated because a broken API boundary does not just return an HTTP error — it interrupts a multi-step reasoning loop mid-execution, potentially leaving the agent in an inconsistent state with no clear recovery path. Catching these breaks in CI before deployment is therefore significantly more valuable than in conventional microservice architectures.
Benefits
Prevents breaking changes from reaching production: the provider cannot deploy a version that fails its consumers' pacts.
Consumer-driven ownership: the consuming team (agent developers) define what they need; the provider team verifies they deliver it.
Version-aware: the Pact Broker tracks which consumer version is compatible with which provider version, enabling precise can-i-deploy decisions.
Reduces E2E test scope: once API contracts are verified, E2E tests can focus on behavior rather than API structure.
Enables independent deployment: teams can deploy consumers and providers independently as long as verified pacts exist for the target version combination.
Fast feedback: provider verification runs in seconds against a running local server, not a full staging environment.
Trade-offs
PACT testing adds a new artifact (the pact file) and a new service (the Pact Broker) to the CI pipeline.
Pacts must be updated when the consumer intentionally changes how it uses the provider — they are living documents, not set-and-forget.
Provider verification requires running the provider application (or a test harness) in CI, adding setup complexity.
PACT is designed for synchronous HTTP interactions; it requires extension (Message Pact, Async Pact) for event-driven or streaming interfaces.
Teams without strong CI discipline may let pact verification failures accumulate, undermining the guarantee.
How it works
Step 1: Consumer test generates a pact

The consumer team writes a test in which the agent orchestrator calls a mock provider server (provided by the PACT library). The test defines the expected interaction: the request method, path, headers, and body; and the expected response status, headers, and body. When the test passes, the PACT library writes the interaction to a pact JSON file.
Step 2: Pact is published to the Pact Broker

The CI pipeline publishes the generated pact file to a Pact Broker instance (self-hosted or via PactFlow, the managed service). The pact is tagged with the consumer's application version (typically the git SHA) and branch.
Step 3: Provider fetches and verifies the pact

The provider's CI pipeline is triggered either by a webhook from the Pact Broker (when a new pact is published) or on a scheduled check. The provider starts its application in a test mode, fetches all pacts from the Broker, and replays each interaction against its running server. If all interactions produce responses that match the consumer's expectations, the verification passes.
Step 4: can-i-deploy gate

Before deploying either the consumer or the provider to any environment, the CI pipeline calls the Pact Broker's can-i-deploy command. This checks that the version being deployed has verified pacts against all its consumer or provider counterparts that are already deployed to that environment. If any pact is unverified or failed verification, can-i-deploy returns a non-zero exit code and blocks the deployment.
Architecture

attrs.alt: PACT contract testing flow for agentic AI: consumer publishes pact to broker, provider fetches and verifies, CI gate enforces can-i-deploy

content.text: The diagram shows the five-step PACT workflow. The consumer contract test generates a pact and publishes it to the Pact Broker. The provider fetches the pact, verifies it against its implementation, and publishes the verification result. The CI gate calls can-i-deploy before any deployment, blocking promotion if any verification is missing or failed.
When to use it
Use PACT between any two services that communicate over HTTP where both teams deploy independently.
Use it when the tool service API is versioned and multiple consumer versions may be in use simultaneously.
Use it when the LLM proxy or gateway is maintained by a separate team from the orchestrator.
Do not use it for intra-process function calls or for provider-side contracts against external third-party APIs you do not control — PACT tests internal API boundaries, not external ones.
Avoid it for streaming or WebSocket endpoints without the appropriate PACT extension support.
How to implement

The implementation spans three repositories: the consumer (agent orchestrator), the provider (tool service), and optionally shared PACT tooling. The Pact Broker is either self-hosted (open-source) or managed via PactFlow.

# consumer/tests/pact/test_search_tool_pact.py
# Generates a pact for the agent orchestrator's calls to the search tool service

import json
import pytest
from pact import Consumer, Provider
from myagent.tool_client import SearchToolClient

PACT_DIR = "tests/pact/contracts"

@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
def pact():
    """Create and start a mock provider server for the consumer test."""
    pact = (
        Consumer("agent-orchestrator")
        .has_pact_with(Provider("search-tool-service"), pact_dir=PACT_DIR)
    )
    pact.start_service()
    yield pact
    pact.stop_service()

def test_search_tool_returns_results(pact):
    expected_request = {
        "method": "POST",
        "path": "/search",
        "headers": {"Content-Type": "application/json"},
        "body": {"query": "IPCC sea level rise", "max_results": 5},
    }
    expected_response = {
        "status": 200,
        "headers": {"Content-Type": "application/json"},
        "body": {
            "results": [
                {
                    "title": "IPCC AR6 Sea Level Rise",
                    "url": "https://example.com/ipcc-ar6",
                    "snippet": "Sea levels are projected to rise...",
                }
            ],
            "total_count": 1,
        },
    }

    (
        pact
        .given("the search index contains IPCC documents")
        .upon_receiving("a search request for sea level rise")
        .with_request(**expected_request)
        .will_respond_with(**expected_response)
    )

    with pact:
        client = SearchToolClient(base_url=pact.uri)
        result = client.search(query="IPCC sea level rise", max_results=5)
        assert len(result["results"]) >= 1
        assert "title" in result["results"][0]

# provider/tests/pact/test_search_tool_provider_verification.py
# Verifies that the search tool service satisfies all consumer pacts

import pytest
from pact import Verifier

PACT_BROKER_URL = "https://pact-broker.internal.example.com"
PROVIDER_BASE_URL = "http://localhost:8080"

@pytest.fixture(scope="session", autouse=True)
def start_provider(app_server):
    """app_server is a pytest fixture that starts the real provider app on port 8080."""
    yield

def test_provider_satisfies_consumer_pacts(provider_version, provider_branch):
    verifier = Verifier(provider="search-tool-service", provider_base_url=PROVIDER_BASE_URL)

    output, _ = verifier.verify_with_broker(
        broker_url=PACT_BROKER_URL,
        provider_version=provider_version,
        provider_version_branch=provider_branch,
        publish_verification_results=True,
        enable_pending=True,  # non-blocking for WIP pacts
    )
    assert output == 0, f"Pact verification failed. Exit code: {output}"

# CI pipeline step: can-i-deploy check before production deployment
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

CONSUMER="agent-orchestrator"
CONSUMER_VERSION="${GIT_SHA}"
ENVIRONMENT="production"
PACT_BROKER_URL="https://pact-broker.internal.example.com"

echo "Checking if ${CONSUMER} version ${CONSUMER_VERSION} can deploy to ${ENVIRONMENT}..."

npx pact-broker can-i-deploy \
  --pacticipant "${CONSUMER}" \
  --version "${CONSUMER_VERSION}" \
  --to-environment "${ENVIRONMENT}" \
  --broker-base-url "${PACT_BROKER_URL}" \
  --broker-token "${PACT_BROKER_TOKEN}"

echo "can-i-deploy check passed. Proceeding with deployment."
Examples
The following example shows a provider state handler — the mechanism by which provider verification tests set up the required database or service state before replaying each consumer interaction.
Real-world example

A legal-tech AI platform operates an agent orchestrator that integrates with four internal tool microservices: a case-law search service, a document parser service, a citation extractor, and a summary generator. Before adopting PACT, a routine refactor of the case-law search API — renaming the response field results to documents — caused a silent failure in the agent that only surfaced during user acceptance testing two weeks later.

After adopting PACT, the consumer contract test for the search API explicitly expressed that the agent expected a field named results. The provider's verification step immediately failed when the rename was introduced in the provider's PR. The provider team either reverted the rename, versioned the API with backward compatibility, or coordinated with the consumer team to update the pact first — preventing the regression from reaching any shared environment.
Common mistakes

Writing pacts that are too specific: matching exact values (e.g., an exact snippet string) makes pacts brittle. Use PACT matchers (like, eachLike, regex) to match structure and type rather than exact content.
Not publishing pacts from CI: pacts generated only locally never reach the Broker and provide no deployment gate.
Skipping provider state handlers: provider verification tests fail if the expected state (e.g., a document in the index) has not been set up.
Not enabling pending pacts: without the enable_pending flag, a new consumer pact immediately breaks the provider's CI before the provider team has had a chance to implement the change.
Treating can-i-deploy as optional: if deployment pipelines bypass the can-i-deploy check, contract tests provide no deployment protection.
One pact file per consumer-provider pair, not per endpoint: PACT accumulates all interactions for a pair in a single pact document; splitting by endpoint creates management overhead.
Best practices
Use PACT matchers (like, eachLike, regex, integer, string) instead of exact values so pacts test structure and types, not content.
Publish pacts from CI using the git SHA as the consumer version and the branch name as the version tag.
Configure provider webhooks in the Pact Broker so provider CI is triggered automatically when a new consumer pact is published.
Use the pending pacts feature so newly added pacts do not break the provider's CI before the provider has implemented the change.
Tag pacts with environment labels (main, staging, production) to enable environment-aware can-i-deploy checks.
Review consumer pacts in pull requests — pact changes represent API contract changes and deserve the same review rigor as code changes.
Performance & scalability

PACT tests are fast by design: the consumer test runs against a local mock server with no network latency; the provider verification test runs against a local application instance. The primary overhead is starting the provider application, which typically takes 5–30 seconds. With a well-tuned test fixture, provider verification for 20 consumer interactions completes in under 60 seconds.

At scale, the Pact Broker becomes the central coordination point for all contract relationships. PactFlow (the managed service) supports hundreds of consumer-provider relationships with webhook fanout and parallel verification. Self-hosted Pact Broker instances should be provisioned with a dedicated database and deployed as a highly available service if contract testing is a release gate.
Tooling & ecosystem
pact-python
: Python client library for writing consumer tests and provider verification tests.
pact-jvm
: Java/Kotlin client for JVM-based tool services.
pact-js
: Node.js client for JavaScript/TypeScript services.
Pact Broker (open source)
: self-hosted contract storage, verification tracking, and can-i-deploy endpoint.
PactFlow
: managed Pact Broker SaaS with enhanced features (bidirectional contracts, GraphQL support, audit logs).
pact-broker CLI
: command-line tool for publishing pacts, recording deployments, and running can-i-deploy checks from CI scripts.

Security & reliability

The Pact Broker contains the API contracts for all internal services — it is a sensitive artifact. Access to the Broker should be controlled via API tokens scoped per service. Consumer publish tokens should only allow publishing pacts for the consumer's own name; provider tokens should only allow publishing verification results for the provider's own name. Admin tokens should be restricted to CI automation accounts.

Pact files may contain sample request bodies with realistic-looking data. Ensure that sample data in pacts uses fictional or anonymized values, not real user data. Provider state setup code in verification tests must not write to production databases — it must operate against a test-isolated data store.
Key takeaways
PACT makes implicit API contracts between agentic AI services explicit, version-controlled, and machine-verifiable.
The consumer team generates the contract from their own test cases; the provider verifies it against their real implementation.
The Pact Broker stores contracts and verification results, enabling can-i-deploy to block deployments where the contract is unverified.
Use PACT matchers (not exact values) so contracts test structure and types rather than brittle content.
The pending pacts feature allows new contracts to be introduced without immediately breaking the provider's CI.
Provider webhooks automate the feedback loop: new consumer pacts trigger provider verification automatically.
For agentic AI, PACT is most valuable at tool service boundaries where a schema change can silently break a multi-step agent loop.
